Okay, welcome back. Now, let's talk about making these suckers work real good. That's the technical term, real good. So I'm gonna start by messing with, yeah, that's another technical term, the two campaigns that we built together last week. And so we can see them here in the high-rose. Alright, here's one and this is like, you know, again, we just kind of saw this earlier. Here's one for our date range right here. So this is when we built them. And now both of these have done pretty good, you know, and we can see that our one where we had 20 different audiences and we were testing that classic method, that worked pretty good. Everything's still at five bucks a day. Alright, my high-rose sit too long so it's refreshing everything. Let's see if we're still for the same date range. We are good. Alright, so everything's still, as you can see, at five bucks a day over here in this big campaign. And it's also still at five bucks a day in this other campaign where we're just targeting four audiences but they're just like literally, you know, everybody between 34 and 55 across these different countries and stuff. So as you can tell, some of these audiences worked pretty good and made us a bunch of money and some of them didn't. So the question is, well, what do we do? Now, obviously the ones where we turned a profit, we want to scale these. And the ones where we didn't turn a profit, we want to turn some but not all of them off. And the first thing I want to do is talk to you about scaling. Alright, so there's two types of scaling. There is vertical. Let me get my pen working here. Okay, there's vertical and that's a super pen. And then there is horizontal. Alright, and so vertical scaling means this. We have an audience that works. Let's say it's this audience right here, wealth, we've made it 2400% ROI, 25x ROAS. Ridiculous. Totally didn't think that audience would work, by the way. Just guessed. You know, sometimes you just pull them out of your ear and they work. What do you know? So okay, spent a freaking $37 and made $954. I'll take it. Is this going to hold forever? No, but I definitely want to scale this. So an example of vertical scaling is where we take an audience, alright, one audience, and we just start adding to the budget every day. So instead of $5, I go up to $10 and tomorrow I go to $15. Next day I go to $20. Next day I go to $30, etc. Right, and so that's an example of vertical scaling. An example of horizontal scaling is where I take this audience, right, so what was it? Wealth, right? So I take that wealth audience. And one example is where I build a new campaign and I duplicate it, but instead of using that bidding strategy, I use a different bidding strategy. And if you remember, I told you there were like the components, the building box of ads. So some of those building blocks are bidding strategies. So I could just duplicate a new campaign to create a new campaign with this audience and I could use a new bid strategy. So I'll put this strategy, right, and or I could use a new ad or creative. It's the same term by the same thing. Creative ad is the same thing. So instead of video ad, I could use an image ad, right? Another way to define horizontal scaling is I keep finding more audiences that work, right? So here's what's definitely going to happen, alright? Let's say we have this one audience, wealth, alright? And let's say I start scaling this thing up and I get it to like a hundred bucks a day or whatever. And between like, you know, just adding to the budget in this campaign for this audience right here and between like building another campaign, where's my whiteboard, building another campaign or I'm using a different strategy, like a cost cap strategy and or building another campaign where I'm, you know, using that audience with a different ad like a video ad instead. You know, let's say I get that one audience to where I'm spending a thousand dollars a day, okay? Between like both of or all of these different methodologies. Eventually, this is going to stop scaling. The performance is going to go down, alright? And that's normal. That's why the very first video that I made us for this week was like, okay, look, when things quit working, here's what to do. So it's definitely going to like stop working eventually, right? It doesn't mean when it does stop working, it doesn't mean it's going to stop working forever. Like I mentioned, there's these four things we can do to revive it. And typically one of those four things always works. Alright? But if we rely strictly on like just finding a handful of audiences and always scaling those, we're going to be in trouble. So another definition of horizontal scaling is we've got that audience of wealth that's working, right? So we have wealth, yay. And then we also have right here, we have HubSpot, alright? And so now we have HubSpot. This is cool. And so these two are like both so exceptionally good that the profit from these two audiences has more than made up for the combined losses of all of the other audiences, which is ridiculous, right? So like, you know, usually it's not this dramatic. Usually when you test like 20, you might have five of them that turn a profit, the other 15 don't. But whatever, we could keep doing this methodology because this turn to profit, this whole test turn to profit until we found like literally, you know, and we always really should be doing this method methodology until we've got, you know, wealth HubSpot, internet marketing, small business, you know, social media, we have a look alike audience in there, etc. Right? So when you have a gazillion different audiences and they're all at, you know, a lower budget or whatever, that's called horizontal scaling, right? That's really a better definition of horizontal scaling. So the first thing we want to do our first step in moving forward in terms of like, Hey, what's next with this particular campaign is we just want to do plain and simple horizontal scaling. We want to take what's working and we want to spend more money on it and we want to take what's not working and we want to turn it off. The question becomes, how do we define what is not working? So now, you know, my ad expense has gone from $100 a day for this campaign down to $35 a day, which is good. So now what do I do with these guys? You know, I'm going to raise the budgets on them. And so here's the thing about raising the budgets. There are multiple ways you can do it. If you want to do it like the safest way, then I would raise this thing 25% per day until it breaks. All right. And when it breaks, I would leave it alone for three days and then see if it doesn't improve, you know, and I would do this with the understanding that not all of these over the next, you know, $35 and spend per audience, they're not all going to remain profitable. Some of them might crap out on you and that's totally normal. That's fine. You know, so in this case, you know, if we raise a $5 budget by 25% per day, we're going to go from $5 to six. So I'm just going to double it. And I'm going to go to 10 bucks here. All right. So I'm just going to come over here. I'm selecting them all. And I'm going to click on edit. All right. And because they all have the same budget now, I can literally just change the budget of each one of them from $5 to $10. All right. So that one is now going to be doubled. Okay. So we've doubled this one. All right.
